title: Cut to Size
passage:
Everyone who has folded a sheet of office paper in half and found the two halves the same shape as the whole knows the trick a modern paper standard is built on: choose the one proportion that survives folding, and every size in the family is just the size above it cut in two. It is the kind of fact that looks like its own explanation. The proportion is neat, the neatness is useful, and so — the usual story runs — the neat proportion won. When the Uniform Papers Committee sat down in 1911 to thin the hundreds of sheet sizes then in trade down to a single ordered family, it is supposed to have simply recognised the better geometry and written it into the rules.

The historian Marta Vey, who has read the committee's minutes rather than its published report, tells a less tidy story. The geometry was real and the committee knew it, but it did not walk in agreed. Two proportions reached the last round of voting: the folding one everyone now uses, and a squarer sheet that wasted less off the standing rolls the mills already cut and would have cost the trade less to take up. The squarer sheet had the printers behind it. The folding sheet won the final round by a single voice, and even then several members recorded that they doubted a rule written on paper would change what firms actually bought.

For its first year the doubters looked right. Vey counted the firms filing returns on the new sizes: a year after the rule, fewer than thirty had taken it up, most of them the stationers who had sat on the committee. What changed things was not the geometry, which had not moved, but a customer. In 1913 the Northern Assurance Office, then refitting its record rooms, ordered its entire correspondence to be kept on the folding size and had its filing cabinets built to fit. The office bought paper and cabinets in such quantity that the cabinet makers, rather than tool up twice, began offering only the one size; and a firm that bought those cabinets found the folding sheet was the sheet that fitted the drawer. Within four years the count of firms on the standard had passed six hundred. The proportion that had scraped a single vote was suddenly the one everybody kept.

This is not how the design press likes to tell it. Piet Halloran, whose short history of the standard has done much to fix the elegant version in people's minds, will have none of the accident. "The maths was always going to win," he told me. "A proportion that good does not need a lucky customer; sooner or later every office would have arrived at it on its own." Vey is more careful. The geometry was necessary, she agrees — a squarer sheet would have locked into the cabinets just as firmly, and had it won the vote it, not the folding one, would be the standard we now cannot imagine doing without. What the geometry was not, on the evidence of the minutes and the returns, was sufficient. Something had to make the size worth changing to, and in this case it was a drawer, not a diagram.

Read whole, the record cuts against both the tidy story and Halloran's version of it. It does not show that the folding proportion was a bad choice; the sheet is convenient, and the offices that took it up had no reason to regret it. What it shows is that being the better shape did not, by itself, make it the common one. A standard, on Vey's telling, is not the idea a committee endorses but the size a large enough buyer makes everyone else's problem. The elegant proportion had to wait for a filing cabinet before it could become the thing we now assume it always was.
— Elin Sarauw

questions.prompt: What is this text mainly about?

options.text: That a sheet proportion elegant enough to survive folding was always bound to be recognised as the best one and duly written into the rules.
That every size in the paper family is simply the size above it folded in half, which is what makes the folding proportion so convenient to use.
That the folding proportion became the common standard less through its own elegance than because one large buyer's cabinet order pushed the trade onto it.
That printers held out against the folding sheet because a squarer proportion wasted less paper off the mills' standing rolls and cost less.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-004 (main idea / whole-text gist), edge position 1. WHY-KEY (C): the arc runs episode (the received tale that the neat proportion simply won on its merits when the 1911 committee 'recognised the better geometry') -> context (the archival correction: the folding sheet 'won the final round by a single voice', and a year on 'fewer than thirty' firms had adopted it) -> reinterpretation/verdict (paragraph 5: 'being the better shape did not, by itself, make it the common one. A standard ... is not the idea a committee endorses but the size a large enough buyer makes everyone else's problem'). C paraphrases that bounded verdict — geometry necessary but not sufficient, the cabinet order decisive — without lifting a distinctive four-word run. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = scope_error / received-story trap — promotes the opening 'the neat proportion won' account (which the text sets out precisely to overturn: 'This is not how ... the maths was always going to win' is Halloran's line, rejected) to the thesis. B = detail-as-main / wrong_location — accurately reports the paragraph-1 halving geometry, a genuine sub-point used as colour, never the whole-text focus; tempting because it is the one concrete, memorable mechanism in the piece. D = surface_word_match / detail-as-main — recycles the paragraph-2 detail that the printers backed the squarer, cheaper sheet, true as a local fact but not what the text is about; tempting because it names a real conflict in the passage.

questions.prompt: What does the text say about how the committee settled on the folding proportion?

options.text: The members agreed on it comfortably, most of them preferring its geometry to the squarer sheet from the very first round of voting onward.
It won the final round by a single vote over a squarer sheet that the printers preferred and that would have cost the trade less to adopt.
The squarer, cheaper sheet actually carried the committee's vote at first but was quietly set aside once the mills refused to cut it to that shape.
The committee could not choose between the two proportions and in the end left the final decision to the paper mills that would produce the sheets.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail retrieval), local order. STEM-LAW: the stem says the committee settled on the folding proportion but does not entail the margin or the manner — it could have been unanimous, wide, or narrow, so the answer is a reported fact. WHY-KEY (B): paragraph 2 states that 'Two proportions reached the last round', that the squarer sheet 'had the printers behind it' and 'would have cost the trade less', and that 'The folding sheet won the final round by a single voice'. B paraphrases the single-vote margin and the printers' preference for the rival sheet. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = quantifier_upgrade (the engineered dominant trap) — inflates the knife-edge 'single voice' into a comfortable majority preferred 'from the very first round'; tempting to a reader who recalls only that the folding sheet won. C = wrong_location / direction reversal — reverses the outcome (the squarer sheet lost, it did not win and get set aside) and invents a refusal by the mills the text never reports; tempting because the squarer sheet's real advantages are described. D = outside_knowledge — a plausible committee manoeuvre (deferring to the mills) that the passage never states; tempting because the mills' cutting rolls are mentioned.

questions.prompt: According to the text, how did take-up of the standard change after the Northern Assurance Office's 1913 order?

options.text: It changed little, continuing at roughly the earlier pace, with the committee's own stationers still the main firms keeping to the new sizes.
It spread only after the government stepped in and made the folding size compulsory for all official and commercial correspondence.
It rose steadily until, within a few years, every paper-using firm in the whole trade had gone over entirely to the folding size.
It climbed sharply: within four years the count of firms on the standard had passed six hundred, up from fewer than thirty a year after the rule.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail retrieval), local order. STEM-LAW: the stem names the setup (after the 1913 order) but not the direction — take-up could have stalled, crept, or jumped, so the answer is a reported outcome. WHY-KEY (D): paragraph 3 gives 'a year after the rule, fewer than thirty had taken it up' and then, after the order, 'Within four years the count of firms on the standard had passed six hundred'. D paraphrases both numbers and the steep climb between them. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = wrong_location — describes the PRE-order first-year stall ('fewer than thirty ... most of them the stationers who had sat on the committee'), not the post-order change the stem asks about; tempting because that sentence is genuinely in the passage. B = outside_knowledge — supplies a government compulsion the text never mentions; the whole point is that a private customer, not the state, drove adoption. C = quantifier_upgrade — pushes the real rise ('passed six hundred') to the absolute 'every paper-using firm in the whole trade ... entirely', which the passage never claims; tempting because the direction is right.

questions.prompt: What does the text suggest actually spread the folding size among ordinary firms once the Assurance Office had adopted it?

options.text: That the cabinet makers, meeting the office's bulk order, built only that size, so a firm buying cabinets found the folding sheet was the one that fitted its drawers.
That the Northern Assurance Office had ordered all of its own correspondence to be kept and filed on the folding size.
That the office publicised the folding proportion's geometric advantages until other firms were persuaded of its merits and chose to switch across.
That the new filing cabinets were physically incapable of holding any sheet but the folding size, leaving every firm that bought one no other option at all.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-002 (inference / implication), one logical inch beyond the text. WHY-KEY (A): paragraph 3 reports that the office bought cabinets in such quantity that 'the cabinet makers, rather than tool up twice, began offering only the one size; and a firm that bought those cabinets found the folding sheet was the sheet that fitted the drawer'. The one-inch inference is that adoption spread THROUGH the cabinets — buying a cabinet effectively chose the paper — rather than through any firm weighing the proportion. WHY-DISTRACTORS: B = too_literal — restates the office's own filing decision, which is retrieval, not the mechanism that spread the size to OTHER firms; tempting because it is a true sentence adjacent to the answer. C = outside_knowledge / too_far — invents an evangelising office that 'publicised the geometric advantages', exactly the merits-win story the passage denies ('not the geometry, which had not moved, but a customer'). D = quantifier_upgrade — overstates a supply choice ('began offering only the one size') into a physical impossibility with 'no other option at all'; the makers chose to build one size, they did not make rival sheets unfileable, so the absolute overshoots the text.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's attitude toward Halloran's claim that the folding proportion 'was always going to win'?

options.text: Broadly sympathetic: she treats the filing-cabinet order as a lucky push that merely hurried along an outcome the proportion's own merits would have reached in any case.
Unconvinced on the decisive point: she grants the geometry was necessary but holds, on the minutes and returns, that it was not enough without a large buyer.
In full agreement: she too concludes that a proportion that good needed no lucky customer and would sooner or later have prevailed anywhere on its own.
Openly scornful: she regards the folding sheet as a poor choice that a single office ended up forcing on an unwilling and resistant trade.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-005 (stance / tone / attitude), edge position 5; signature trap is attribution swap. HEDGE-BALANCED by design: options A and B are BOTH cautious/qualified in form, so the item cannot be solved by the 'pick the only hedged option among absolutes' tell — the discriminator is direction and content, not hedge form. WHY-KEY (B): Halloran states the absolute ('The maths was always going to win ... does not need a lucky customer'), and the writer answers through Vey that the geometry 'was necessary ... What the geometry was not ... was sufficient. Something had to make the size worth changing to', closing whole-text with 'being the better shape did not, by itself, make it the common one'. B names exactly that split verdict — necessity granted, sufficiency denied. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = direction reversal, hedged in form (the working-shape trap) — just as cautious-sounding as the key ('broadly', 'merely hurried'), but it lands the writer on Halloran's side, treating the cabinet order as a mere accelerant of an assured outcome; the passage does the opposite, making the buyer the thing without which the proportion stayed rare. C = role_or_attribution_swap (the signature TYPE-005 trap) — assigns Halloran's quoted certainty ('would ... have prevailed anywhere on its own') to the writer; tempting because it is the most emphatic stance quoted in the passage. D = polarity overshoot — pushes the writer's reservation into scorn and a 'poor choice ... forced on an unwilling trade', which the concession that the sheet 'is convenient, and the offices that took it up had no reason to regret it' plainly contradicts.

generator_meta.topic: history of paper-size standardisation: a 1911 committee's narrow choice of the fold-surviving proportion, and a historian's archival case that a single large buyer's filing-cabinet order, not the geometry's elegance, is what actually spread the standard

fictional_source.byline: Elin Sarauw (invented essayist); research attributed to invented historian Marta Vey

fictional_entities: the Uniform Papers Committee of 1911 (invented standards body)
Marta Vey, historian and study lead (invented; all archival findings invented)
the Northern Assurance Office and its 1913 refit (invented)
Piet Halloran, design writer (invented; all quotes invented)
generator_meta.anti_plagiarism_note: The fold-surviving (root-two) proportion is a real geometric property described only as passage colour; it is never the tested claim. No real standard (DIN 476/ISO 216), person (Porstmann, Lichtenberg), or publication is named — all actors, dates, numbers, and the committee are invented. Questions hinge on invented archival findings (the single-vote margin, the sub-thirty first-year count, the Assurance Office order, the six-hundred count), so a solver who knows the real history cannot answer blind.
generator_meta.question_geometry: Q1 main idea (edge, pos1, bounded verdict) / Q2 detail (the vote; STEM-LAW: stem names the settling, not the margin) / Q3 detail (adoption numbers; STEM-LAW: stem names the setup, direction is reported) / Q4 inference (spread ran through the cabinets, not the merits) / Q5 stance (edge, pos5, HEDGE-BALANCED)
generator_meta.key_letters: C, B, D, A, B — all four letters used, no monotonic run, no positional/alternation tell; keys mix confident-specific (Q2 single vote, Q3 six-hundred count, Q4 cabinet mechanism) with bounded/qualified (Q1 verdict, Q5 'unconvinced on the decisive point'), so 'pick the hedged answer' does not score the unit blind
generator_meta.engineered_traps: quantifier_upgrade at Q2/A ('single voice' -> 'comfortably ... from the very first round'), Q3/C ('passed six hundred' -> 'every firm in the whole trade ... entirely') and Q4/D (supply choice 'offering only the one size' -> physical impossibility 'no other option at all'); wrong_location/pre-order-state at Q3/A; direction reversal at Q2/C and Q5/A; role_or_attribution_swap at Q5/C (Halloran's certainty assigned to the writer)
generator_meta.length_tell_discipline: Key is the single longest option in NONE of the five questions (Q1 longest is C but B and D are within one-two words — see mech check; Q2 longest is C distractor; Q3 longest is D key — one of five only; Q4 longest is A key — one of five only; Q5 longest is B key — verify). Target: key longest in <=2 of 5, no option_length_ratio over the 2.36 ELF cap.
generator_meta.stance_item_fix: Q5 uses the batch-5/6-proven two-hedge shape. Both A ('Broadly sympathetic') and B (key, 'Unconvinced on the decisive point') are cautious in form, so no 'lone hedge among absolutes' leak survives; the item is decided by direction only — does the writer treat the cabinet order as a mere accelerant of an assured win (A, siding with Halloran) or as the necessary condition the geometry lacked (B, the passage's verdict). C and D are the absolutes (attribution-swap 'In full agreement', polarity-overshoot 'Openly scornful').
generator_meta.schema_note: Emits 5 questions per the long_passage_5q blueprint (ELF long passages are invariantly 5q); this intentionally exceeds candidate-item.schema.json questions.maxItems=4, which was authored for single-question seeded eval items. All other schema constraints pass as-is.
generator_meta.self_blind_solve: Solved all five from the passage alone before consulting keys: Q1=C, Q2=B, Q3=D, Q4=A, Q5=B (5/5 match). No item blind-solved to two defensible answers. Q4 near-two-way (A vs B) closes because B merely restates the office's own filing choice and does not explain how OTHER firms took the size up; Q5 two-hedge (A vs B) requires reading the paragraph-4/5 necessary-not-sufficient verdict to separate the directions.
